nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; benthic species inhabiting rubble and algal-covered patches of dead reef in 1–6 m depth

diet.notes: Grazes on detritus and microcrustaceans such as copepods and amphipods in its natural habitat. Has been documented accepting prepared aquarium foods as well as live offerings; a refugium-seeded copepod population is generally advised to supplement feeding.

summary: A tiny captive-bred reef goby from the Western Pacific, generally considered peaceful and reef-safe. Tends to form small shoals, grazes detritus and copepods, and is well-suited to nano and pico reef tanks.
careNotes:
The Queensland Dwarf Goby is one of the smallest gobies available in the aquarium hobby, typically reaching around 2.4 cm as an adult. It is generally considered reef-safe and peaceful toward corals and most invertebrates. Captive-bred specimens (such as those from the BIOTA program) tend to acclimate to aquarium life more readily than wild-caught individuals.

This species has been observed forming small shoals in the wild among coral rubble, and captive groups are reported to coexist peacefully — a notable trait among gobies. Ideal for nano and pico reef setups as well as established reef aquariums. A healthy population of copepods and amphipods is beneficial; a refugium connected to the display tank can help sustain this.

Due to its very small size, care should be taken to protect against strong filter intakes (sponge pre-filters recommended). A tightly-fitted lid or mesh cover is advisable, as gobies of this genus tend to jump. Tank minimum is set at 75 L cautiously, to support a small group with adequate territory.

The lifespan of Eviota gobies is notably short by aquarium standards. FishBase records a generation time of approximately 0.2 years (~73 days), consistent with published studies on the Eviota genus showing maximum lifespans of roughly 59–90 days in the wild. Captive specimens may live somewhat longer under stable conditions, but aquarists should expect a short-lived fish and not interpret natural senescence as disease.

Temperature: AlgaeBarn reported ~78°F (25.6°C); FishBase wild-population data gives 26.4–29.3°C. The averaged range of 26–29°C is used here. Salinity consensus across sources was 1.021–1.026 SG. Adult size: AlgaeBarn states ~1 inch (~2.54 cm), FishBase records 2.4 cm SL; range 1.5–2.4 cm reflects this spread.
breedingNotes: Spawning in Eviota species has been documented to follow semilunar or 14-day cycles. Captive breeding information for Eviota queenslandica specifically is not well-documented in available hobby sources.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source URL was a collection page; Queensland Dwarf Goby was not listed. Data sourced from secondary and tertiary sources instead.

additional.name: algaebarn.com
additional.notes: Provided size (~1 inch), temperature (~78°F/25.6°C), pH (8.0–8.4), dKH (8–12), salinity (1.021–1.023 SG), diet notes, shoaling behavior, reef-safe status, and captive-bred availability.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Provided scientific taxonomy (Gobiidae, Gobiiformes), adult size (2.4 cm SL), temperature range (26.4–29.3°C), native range (Western Pacific: southwestern Thailand to Vanuatu, north to southern Taiwan, south to the southern Great Barrier Reef), habitat (rubble and algal-covered patches of dead reef at 1–6 m depth), diet (small invertebrates and detritus), and generation time (~0.2 years). Cross-checked sizing and water parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Semilunar spawning cycles documented in wild populations. Captive breeding is not well-documented for this species in the hobby.
